statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, implement the merge sort algorithm to sort the array in ascending order. The merge sort algorithm is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that divides the array into two halves, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves.

constraints.description: The length of the input array.
The range of values in the input array.

examples.explanation: The input array [4, 2, 7, 1, 3] is sorted in ascending order to produce [1, 2, 3, 4, 7].
The input array [3, 2, 1] is sorted in ascending order to produce [1, 2, 3].
The input array [5] is already sorted, so the output is [5].
The input array is empty, so the output is empty.

hints.text: The merge sort algorithm is a divide-and-conquer algorithm. Think about how you can divide the input array into smaller sub-problems.
The algorithm recursively sorts each half of the array. Consider how you can use recursion to solve this problem.
After sorting each half, you need to merge them. Think about how you can compare elements from each half and combine them into a single sorted array.

editorial.title: Efficient Sorting with Merge Sort
editorial.intuition: The key observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that sorting can be achieved by dividing the array into smaller subarrays, sorting them individually, and then merging them back together in sorted order.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve comparing each pair of elements in the array and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2), which is inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to use a divide-and-conquer strategy, dividing the array into smaller subarrays, sorting them recursively, and then merging them back together in sorted order.
editorial.optimalSolution: The merge sort algorithm achieves this by recursively dividing the array into two halves until each subarray has one element, and then merging them back together in sorted order using a helper function.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of merge sort can be proven by induction. The base case is when the array has one element, which is already sorted. For larger arrays, the merge step ensures that the resulting array is sorted by maintaining a sorted prefix and appending the remaining elements in sorted order.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm on a sample input, such as [3, 2, 1], illustrates the recursive division and merging steps: [3, 2, 1] -> [3] + [2, 1] -> [3] + [2] + [1] -> [1, 2, 3].
commonMistakes: Incorrectly implementing the merge step, leading to an unsorted array.
Failing to handle edge cases, such as an empty or single-element array.
Using an inefficient data structure, such as a linked list, which can increase the time complexity.
Not considering the space complexity, which can lead to excessive memory usage.
Incorrectly assuming that the input array is already partially sorted.
